albums.title: OK Computer
albums.reason: With 2050 all-time plays, Radiohead is your second most-played artist ever, and OK Computer is the record that likely started it — worth returning to as a full listen rather than scattered tracks.

albums.title: The Wall
albums.reason: Pink Floyd sits at 1199 all-time plays and you've revisited Animals and Wish You Were Here recently — The Wall is the grand vault pick you probably haven't spun end-to-end in a while.

albums.title: Human After All
albums.reason: Your top artist at 2940 plays, and while Discovery and Homework have been recommended recently, Human After All is the raw, repetitive deep cut that deserves a revisit — Veridis Quo and Digital Love energy taken somewhere darker.

albums.title: Talkie Walkie
albums.reason: Air sits at 1011 all-time plays and Moon Safari has already been surfaced — Talkie Walkie is the quieter, more melancholic sibling that you may not have revisited in years.

albums.title: Sam's Town
albums.reason: 926 all-time plays puts The Killers firmly in your bedrock, and Sam's Town is their sweeping, underappreciated second album — big-hearted and dramatic in all the right ways.

albums.title: Junior
albums.reason: Röyksopp is your 5th most-played artist ever at 1062 plays, and with Melody A.M. and The Understanding already recommended, Junior is the bright, propulsive album in the vault that deserves its turn.

albums.title: AM
albums.reason: 809 all-time plays for Arctic Monkeys and Humbug has been surfaced recently — AM is the slick, nocturnal version of them and a comfort listen you've probably not revisited as a full album lately.

albums.title: Second Toughest in the Infants
albums.reason: Underworld sits at 633 plays with Born Slippy in your top 20 tracks, and Beaucoup Fish and In Sides have already come up — Second Toughest is the deeper, more abstract vault record that rewards a long sit.

albums.title: Movements
albums.reason: Your love of Daft Punk, Justice, and Leftfield points straight at Booka Shade's debut — it sits at that same intersection of electronic muscle and melodic warmth, and Movements is a genuine cult classic in the lane.

albums.title: The Last Resort
albums.reason: Your deep affinity for Radiohead's melancholy and Underworld's hypnotic sprawl makes Trentemøller's debut a natural discovery — cinematic, brooding, and strangely beautiful in the same way your all-time favourites are.

albums.title: Walls
albums.reason: Rooted in the same emotional electronic space as James Blake, Röyksopp, and Air, Walls is a masterclass in restraint — lush, aching, and unlike most things adjacent to your all-time list.

albums.title: Hinterland
albums.reason: Your passion for Orbital, Leftfield, and the deeper end of electronic music lines up perfectly with Recondite's Hinterland — understated, atmospheric, and the kind of record you can disappear into for an hour.

albums.title: Drowning in a Sea of Love
albums.reason: Sitting in the same emotional orbit as Boards of Canada and Daft Punk at their most wistful, Nathan Fake's debut is a cult classic that somehow never found the audience it deserved — an all-time discovery waiting to happen.

albums.title: Chromophobia
albums.reason: If you love the melodic intelligence of Daft Punk and the propulsive build of Justice, Gui Boratto's Chromophobia is the missing link — Brazilian electronic music with a deep pop sensibility, and still criminally underheard.

albums.title: Body Riddle
albums.reason: Your affinity for Squarepusher's energy and Radiohead's experimental side points to Clark's Body Riddle — it's twisted and unpredictable but deeply musical, and a peer to your all-time electronica loves.

albums.title: Isles
albums.reason: Rooted in the same dance music DNA as Daft Punk, Orbital, and Leftfield, Bicep's Isles is the modern record that best captures that feeling — euphoric, melodic, and built for both clubs and headphones.

albums.title: Rigning
albums.reason: Your love of Röyksopp's Scandinavian introspection and Orbital's glacial beauty makes Yagya an ideal discovery — Rigning is a slow, rainy, utterly hypnotic record and a genuine hidden gem in the ambient techno world.

albums.title: Universal Beings
albums.reason: Your recent deep listening to Oscar Peterson Trio and the soul-jazz warmth of Khruangbin points directly at Makaya McCraven — Universal Beings bridges classic jazz instinct with a modern, beat-layered sensibility you'll immediately connect with.

albums.title: Sexuality
albums.reason: Your recent pull toward Khruangbin's globe-trotting warmth and Tosca's languid electronic drift makes Tellier's Sexuality a natural discovery — dreamy, unhurried, and strangely cinematic.

albums.title: Eingya
albums.reason: Given your recent immersion in Nils Frahm, Ben Lukas Boysen, and Brambles' Charcoal, Helios's Eingya is the perfect adjacent discovery — warm, organic, and quietly devastating in the same breath.

albums.title: On the Go
albums.reason: With Bill Withers' soul and Oscar Peterson's jazz both in heavy rotation this year, Matthew Halsall's On the Go feels like a natural bridge — spiritual jazz with a warm, unhurried humanity you'll love.

albums.title: Silence in the Snow
albums.reason: Your recent listening to Nightmares on Wax, Four Tet, and Weval points toward Catching Flies' debut — soft, sun-dappled beats with real emotional weight, sitting in exactly the cosy lo-fi space you've been inhabiting.

albums.title: Quiet Down
albums.reason: Your recent Portico Quartet plays and the jazz-adjacent listening this year makes Slowly Rolling Camera an ideal find — chamber jazz with an electronic undercurrent, sitting beautifully between the worlds you've been exploring.

albums.title: Gold
albums.reason: Your recent Bill Withers and Charles Bradley deep dives, combined with exploratory listens to Sault, makes Gold a perfect next step — quietly radical, warm-hearted, and utterly unlike anything else.

albums.title: Transitions
albums.reason: Given your recent love of Khruangbin's globe-trotting feel and the deep soul of Bill Withers, Rob Araujo's Transitions bridges West African rhythm with jazz harmony in a way that feels like it was made for your current arc.

albums.title: Mordechai
albums.reason: You've played A La Sala and Con Todo El Mundo heavily this month and just ran through A La Sala back-to-back — Mordechai is the other album in the trilogy and rounds out the picture beautifully right now.

albums.title: Art in the Age of Automation
albums.reason: You've been deep in Portico Quartet this week — running through the self-titled album track by track — and Art in the Age of Automation is their more electronic, forward-leaning record that fits exactly where your head is right now.

albums.title: Holism
albums.reason: You've had Gravity and Ben Lukas Boysen in consistent rotation this month — Holism is the companion record that takes his sound into more urgent, orchestrated territory and deserves to sit next to it this week.

albums.title: Felt
albums.reason: Nils Frahm has been a steady presence in your recent month and this week — Felt is the quietest, most intimate thing he's made, recorded with the piano damped at night so as not to wake his neighbours, and it's perfect for right now.

albums.title: Tlapa
albums.reason: Tosca has been a major presence in your recent listening — you've run through Suzuki heavily — and Tlapa is their more recent, languid record that sits in the same golden afternoon space you've been living in.

albums.title: Hoy Como Ayer
albums.reason: You've had Hermanos Gutiérrez appear in your last month's listening alongside Khruangbin — Hoy Como Ayer is their most atmospheric, unhurried record and the perfect companion to the textural, global sound you're deep in right now.

albums.title: Changes
albums.reason: Neal Francis has been a real presence this month — you played Return to Zero heavily — and Changes is his debut, where the New Orleans-flavoured soul piano grooves that made you love him first emerged.

albums.title: Carve the Runes Then Be Content with Silence
albums.reason: Erland Cooper has appeared in your recent month's listening and this atmospheric, orchestral record is his most expansive — it fits the quiet, contemplative mood running through your current week perfectly.

songs.title: No Surprises
songs.reason: With Reckoner already your 3rd most-played track ever and Radiohead at 2050 all-time plays, No Surprises is the other Radiohead song that hits at that same deeply familiar, comfort-food-from-the-vault frequency.

songs.title: Kashmir
songs.reason: Led Zeppelin sits at 964 all-time plays and while The Rain Song and When the Levee Breaks have already come up, Kashmir is the vault masterpiece — epic, repetitive, and unlike anything else they ever made.

songs.title: Retrograde
songs.reason: James Blake is your 17th most-played artist with 637 plays, and Postpone is your most-played track of all time — Retrograde from Overgrown is the other Blake track that carries the same emotional gravity and probably hasn't been played in a while.

songs.title: Song of Life
songs.reason: Leftfield is in your top 6 all-time at 1016 plays and Release the Pressure is your 4th most-played track — Song of Life from Leftism is the other Leftfield peak, a twelve-minute slow-burn that deserves its moment back in the vault rotation.

songs.title: Instant Crush
songs.reason: Daft Punk leads your all-time chart at 2940 plays with six tracks in your top 20 — Instant Crush from Random Access Memories is the deep cut that doesn't get the love it deserves and hasn't come up in any previous recommendations.

songs.title: The Box
songs.reason: Orbital sits at 777 all-time plays and while Halcyon and Remind have already been picked, The Box from In Sides is the centrepiece of their catalogue — dramatic, building, and one of the greatest electronic tracks ever made.

songs.title: Hysteria
songs.reason: Muse clocks 712 all-time plays and Citizen Erased has already been surfaced — Hysteria from Absolution is the other side of them, pure kinetic energy, and a deep-catalogue track you probably haven't blasted in years.

songs.title: While My Guitar Gently Weeps
songs.reason: The Beatles sit at 831 all-time plays and A Day in the Life has already been picked — While My Guitar Gently Weeps is the vault track from the White Album that hits with the same weight, and it's due a revisit.

songs.title: In White Rooms
songs.reason: Rooted in the same driving electronic melodic space as Justice and Daft Punk, In White Rooms from Movements is the kind of peak-moment track your all-time taste has been circling without ever landing on — pure discovery material.

songs.title: Into the Trees
songs.reason: Your deep love of Radiohead's atmospheric side and Underworld's trance-like builds makes this Trentemøller track an ideal discovery — dark, unhurried, and quietly devastating in a way that feels tailor-made for your all-time sensibility.

songs.title: Levo
songs.reason: Your all-time love of Orbital's patience and Röyksopp's Nordic warmth points straight at Recondite's Levo — minimal, misty, and the kind of track that opens something up rather than closing it down.

songs.title: The Sky Was Pink
songs.reason: One of the most beloved underground electronic tracks of the 2000s, The Sky Was Pink sits in the exact space your all-time taste inhabits — melodic, euphoric, and emotionally generous in the way Boards of Canada and Daft Punk at their best always are.

songs.title: Beautiful Life
songs.reason: Your 2940 plays of Daft Punk and deep love of melodic electronic music with emotional intelligence makes Gui Boratto's Beautiful Life an unmissable discovery — euphoric, elegant, and a cult classic that never broke through the way it deserved.

songs.title: Glue
songs.reason: Your all-time love of Daft Punk, Leftfield, and Orbital's rave DNA makes Bicep's Glue an ideal discovery — a perfect distillation of everything great about UK dance music with modern ears, and still fresh every time.

songs.title: Herr Bar
songs.reason: Your all-time affinity for Squarepusher's playfulness and Daft Punk's melodic warmth makes this Clark track from Body Riddle an ideal find — it has all the musical character you love without sounding like anything you already know.

songs.title: Snowflakes and Carwrecks
songs.reason: Your all-time love of Röyksopp's atmospheric Scandinavian introspection and Orbital's patience makes this Yagya track from Rigning an unmissable discovery — slow, beautiful, and unlike almost anything in your current library.

songs.title: Goodbye
songs.reason: Your all-time top 20 includes James Blake's raw emotional electronic work and Röyksopp's warmth — Apparat's Goodbye from Walls occupies that same deeply felt space and is one of the most affecting tracks in this entire lane.

songs.title: Atlantic Black
songs.reason: Your recent Oscar Peterson and Khruangbin listening points to where jazz and groove meet — Atlantic Black from Universal Beings lands exactly there, warm and immediate, like a session you stumbled into rather than a record you put on.

songs.title: Bless This Morning Year
songs.reason: Your recent deep listening to Nils Frahm and Brambles' Charcoal makes this Helios track an ideal discovery — it has the same quality of light, the same emotional openness, and will feel immediately right.

songs.title: Goodbye April
songs.reason: Your year has been bookmarked by Bill Withers' soul warmth and Oscar Peterson's jazz elegance — Goodbye April from On the Go finds the place where those two worlds meet, gentle and unhurried and full of feeling.

songs.title: Golden Archipelago
songs.reason: Your recent listening to Nightmares on Wax and Four Tet's New Energy makes this Catching Flies track a natural next step — soft, glowing, and with the same unhurried warmth that's defined your recent arc.

songs.title: Come and Find Me
songs.reason: Your recent Portico Quartet deep dive and the chamber jazz territory you've been in makes this Slowly Rolling Camera track a perfect discovery — it sits at exactly the intersection of modern jazz and electronic atmosphere you've been exploring.

songs.title: Go Gently
songs.reason: Your recent Sault and Bill Withers listening maps out a desire for music with deep human warmth and social meaning — Go Gently from Gold delivers that in the most disarming way imaginable, tender and quietly radical.

songs.title: Palmares
songs.reason: Your recent Khruangbin and Bill Withers listening signals a pull toward warmth, groove, and global textures — Palmares from Transitions has all of that, grounded in West African rhythm with a jazz harmony you'll find immediately familiar.

songs.title: Divine
songs.reason: Your recent Tosca and Khruangbin listening has been languid and globe-trotting — Divine from Sexuality occupies that same dreamy, unhurried space but with a French cosmic sensibility that feels genuinely new relative to your current arc.

songs.title: Memory Streams
songs.reason: You've run through the self-titled Portico Quartet album this week track by track — Memory Streams from Art in the Age of Automation is the logical next moment, bridging where you've been with where they went next.

songs.title: August Ten
songs.reason: You've just looped A La Sala in full and have been deep in Khruangbin all week — August Ten from Mordechai is one of their most quietly affecting tracks and exactly the mood you're in right now.

songs.title: Strands
songs.reason: Ben Lukas Boysen has been a constant in your recent month — Strands from Holism captures the tension and release that makes his work so compelling and fits the contemplative headspace you've been in.

songs.title: All Melody
songs.reason: Nils Frahm has been consistent across your recent month and week — All Melody (the title track) is one of his most generous, full-bodied pieces and the right song to drop into this particular moment.

songs.title: El Bueno y El Malo
songs.reason: Hermanos Gutiérrez appeared in your recent month alongside Khruangbin, and these two share a textural, cinematic quality — El Bueno y El Malo is their signature track and fits the dusty, unhurried mood running through your whole week.

songs.title: Black Limo
songs.reason: You've had Neal Francis in regular rotation this month and played Return to Zero heavily — Black Limo from Changes is the track that captures his New Orleans soul piano feel at its most irresistible.

songs.title: Solan Goose
songs.reason: Erland Cooper has been a quiet presence in your recent month and this atmospheric track from Solan is the one that best captures the peaceful, spacious quality you've been drawn to across your current listening.

songs.title: Apricot
songs.reason: You've played Tosca heavily this month and Suzuki has been running on repeat — Apricot from Tlapa is a glowing, unhurried track that sits perfectly in the golden, drifting headspace you're currently in.

djSets.setName: HMWL Podcast 430: Niconé & David Hasert (Kompakt / Get Physical / HMWL)
Daphni - FOLD, London. Nov 22, 2025. 7hr DJ set
Klausmarie b2b Alec Troniq - RAVIN' RAVIN' 2026 @ Sektor Evolution
